message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The
                  <jats:italic>effective reproduction number R</jats:italic>
                  is a prominent statistic for inferring the transmissibility of infectious diseases and effectiveness of interventions.
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  purportedly provides an easy-to-interpret threshold for deducing whether an epidemic will grow (
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  >1) or decline (
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <1). We posit that this interpretation can be misleading and statistically overconfident when applied to infections accumulated from groups featuring heterogeneous dynamics. These groups may be delineated by geography, infectiousness or sociodemographic factors. In these settings,
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  implicitly weights the dynamics of the groups by their number of circulating infections. We find that this weighting can cause delayed detection of outbreak resurgence and premature signalling of epidemic control because it underrepresents the risks from highly transmissible groups. Applying
                  <jats:italic>E-optimal</jats:italic>
                  experimental design theory, we develop a weighting algorithm to minimise these issues, yielding the
                  <jats:italic>risk averse reproduction number E</jats:italic>
                  . Using simulations, analytic approaches and real-world COVID-19 data stratified at the city and district level, we show that
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  meaningfully summarises transmission dynamics across groups, balancing bias from the averaging underlying
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  with variance from directly using local group estimates. An
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  >1 generates timely resurgence signals (upweighting risky groups), while an
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  <1 ensures local outbreaks are under control. We propose
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  as an alternative to
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  for informing policy and assessing transmissibility at large scales (e.g., state-wide or nationally), where
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  is commonly computed but well-mixed or homogeneity assumptions break down.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Author Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    How can we meaningfully summarise the transmission dynamics of an infectious disease? This question, although fundamental to epidemiology and crucial for informing the design and implementation of interventions (e.g., quarantines), is still not resolved. Current practice is to estimate the
                    <jats:italic>effective reproduction number R</jats:italic>
                    , which counts the average number of new infections generated per past infection, at large scales (e.g., nationally). An estimated
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    >1 signals epidemic growth. While
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    is easily interpreted and computed in real time, it averages infections across diverse locations or socio-demographic groups that likely possess different transmission dynamics. We prove that this averaging in
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    reduces sensitivity to resurgence, making
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    >1 slow to reflect realistic epidemic growth. This delay can substantially misinform policymakers and impede interventions. We apply optimal design theory to derive the
                    <jats:italic>risk averse reproduction number E</jats:italic>
                    as an alternative summary of diverse transmission dynamics. Using mathematical arguments, simulations and empirical COVID-19 datasets, we show that
                    <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                    >1 is an improved threshold for resurgence, providing timelier signals for informing policy or interventions and better uncertainty quantification. Further,
                    <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                    maintains the computability and interpretability of
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    . We propose
                    <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                    as meaningful statistic at large scales, where the averaging within
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    likely misrepresents the diversity of transmission dynamics.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Risk averse reproduction numbers improve resurgence detection
